title: practical english usage - Part 281
content:
I know two MP's personally. (More usually:. .. MPs.) It was in the early 1980's. (More usually:...  1980s.) He writes b's instead of d's.

numbers and abbreviations.

Apostrophes are generally used in the plurals of letters, and sometimes of It is a nice idea, but there are a lot of if ’s.

plural form is written.

Words which  do not usually have plurals sometimes have an apostrophe when a 3  special     plurals not Whose house did she stay in? (     Who's...) not The cat had not had its food yet. (     ... it's food...) not This money is yours, (     .. .-your's.) Possessive determiners and pronouns (e.g. yours, its) do not have apostrophes.

the girl's father  Charles's wife my parents' house We   use apostrophes before or after possessive -s (►   123).

it's (= it is/has)   who's (= who  is/has) can't (= cannot)   I'd (= I would/had) Apostrophes replace letters in contracted forms (►   337).

1  missing      letters We   use apostrophes (') for three main reasons.

either singular or plural (e.g. mathematics, politics). For details, ►     117.3.

Many nouns ending in -ies are singular (e.g. physics, athletics). Many common short compounds are now often written ‘ solid’ , with no because of this, people seem to be using hyphens less, especially in compound The  rules about hyphens are complicated, and usage is not very clear. Perhaps 2 Are   hyphens       disappearing?

ticket-office or ex-husband. They  are not the same as dashes (-): ►    297.

Hyphens are the short lines (-) that we  put between words in expressions like 1  What    are   hyphens?

For the use of capitals with East, North, etc, ►    442.

A Midsummer Night's Dream The Spy who Loved Me Gone with the Wind films and plays g   the first word (and often other important words) in the titles of books, I read it in The/the Guardian.

The  article the in such names is usually, but not always, capitalised.

International Herald Tribune New Scientist f the names of newspapers and magazines Catalan cooking  She's Jewish.    He's a Sikh.

He's Russian.    I speak German.  Japanese history groups and religions e nouns and adjectives referring to nationalities and regions, languages, ethnic prime minister elected?) the Prime Minister is attending the summit  (Compare How is the Swedish Colonel Webb  the Managing Director Mr Smith    Professor Blake      Dr Jones d people’ s titles not directly to the scientist Pasteur but Shakespearean drama (    to pasteurise): this refers to a chemical process, c   Words derived from people’ s names have capitals if they refer to the people.

vocabulary •  343    -ise and -ize despise        franchise       televise compromise      exercise       surprise comprise        enterprise      supervise advise        disguise        revise not advertise (     advertize)devise         improvise and American English. Examples: Most words of two syllables, and a few longer words, have -ise in both British realise/realize (BrE)                     realize (AmE) mechanise/mechanize (BrE)          mechanize (AmE) computerise/computerize (BrE)     computerize (AmE) baptise/baptize (BrE)                     baptize (AmE) In American English, -ize is preferred in these cases. Examples: Many English verbs and some nouns can be spelt with either -ise or -ize.
